summary: Martin argues that while the expected proliferation of processor cores that originally motivated interest in functional programming hasn't materialized as dramatically as predicted, FP remains essential for software development. He contends that FP's benefits extend far beyond concurrency—it makes code easier to write, read, test, and understand because programmers don't need to track mutable state. Martin recommends Clojure specifically for its simplicity, JVM interoperability, and compatibility with object-oriented features like dynamic polymorphism, directly refuting the notion that FP and OO are incompatible.
key_points: Moore's Law plateaued around 2000, leading to multi-core processors and renewed interest in FP for handling concurrency safely
The expected explosion of cores (to 1024+) hasn't happened—we've plateaued at 4 cores for years—but FP remains valuable
FP's real benefit is cognitive simplicity: immutable state means fewer balls to juggle, making code easier to write, read, and test
FP and OO are not mutually incompatible—Clojure provides full access to JVM's dynamic polymorphism while maintaining functional purity
Clojure's homoiconicity (code as data) and absurdly simple syntax make it an ideal language for learning FP
money_quotes: If you can't change the state of a variable, you can't have a race condition.
FP is easier to write, easier to read, easier to test, and easier to understand.
When you push an element onto a stack in an FP language, you get a new stack, you don't change the old one.
The programmer has to juggle fewer balls in the air at the same time. There's less to remember. Less to keep track of.
FP and OO are mutually incompatible! Who told you that? That's nonsense!
Just as pushing an integer onto a stack gives you a new stack, when you call a method that adjusts the value of an object, you get a new object instead of changing the old one.
The code is data that the program can manipulate.
Functional programming is important. You should learn it.
key_insight: Functional programming's enduring value lies not in solving the anticipated multi-core crisis, but in the cognitive simplicity that comes from eliminating mutable state—making code fundamentally easier to reason about.
